attributes.stem:
A 27-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with recurrent nosebleeds. She reports spontaneous nosebleeds occurring several times per month for many years. She has no significant medical history and takes paracetamol occasionally for headaches. She does not take any regular medications.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are multiple small, blanching, erythematous lesions over her lips and oral mucosa that rapidly refill when pressure is released.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following features would most strongly support the diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features suggestive of hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT), including recurrent spontaneous epistaxis and characteristic mucocutaneous telangiectases. The diagnosis of HHT is made using the Curacao criteria, which include: spontaneous recurrent epistaxis, multiple telangiectases, visceral arteriovenous malformations, and first-degree relative with HHT.

Family history of similar symptoms would strongly support the diagnosis as HHT follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern. A positive family history would give this patient three out of four Curacao criteria, confirming the diagnosis of definite HHT.

Persistent unilateral nasal obstruction suggests juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma, which typically affects adolescent males and does not cause telangiectases.

Heavy menstrual bleeding is not a characteristic feature of HHT, though gastrointestinal bleeding from visceral telangiectases occurs in approximately 25% of patients.

Regular cocaine use can cause epistaxis through local tissue damage but would not explain the presence of telangiectases.

Seasonal pattern to epistaxis is not typical of HHT. When epistaxis shows seasonal variation, it typically worsens in winter months due to dry air from indoor heating.
learningPoints: Hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT) is diagnosed using the Curacao criteria: epistaxis, telangiectases, visceral lesions, and family history.
Recurrent epistaxis and mucocutaneous telangiectases are the most common presenting features of Hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia.
Hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern.
references: Olitsky S. Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia: Diagnosis and Management. Am Fam Physician. 2010; 82(7):785-790. Available at: https://www.aafp.org/pubs/afp/issues/2010/1001/p785.html

Martins M, de Lima F, Mendonça C et al. Nasopharyngeal angiofibroma: Our experience and literature review. Int Arch Otorhinolaryngol. 2013; 17(1):14-19. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25991988/

McDonald J, Bayrak-Toydemir P, Pyeritz R. Hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia: An overview of diagnosis, management, and pathogenesis. Genetics in Medicine. 2011; 13:607-616. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21546842/
